CORPORATION 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
100 K Street 
Box 807 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Dear Sir: 
February 21, 1978 
SmithKline Corporation is in the process of establishing a system of 
payroll deductions for contributions to the SmithKline Political Action 
Committee, a federal and state political action committee recognized 
and regulated by the Federal Election Commission. 
I am writing to you to find out if your state laws and regulations 
allow the use of the payroll deduction system for SmithKline employees 
residing there. 
Would you be able to furnish me with copies of the 
relevant statutes and/or regulations which govern this method of 
contributing to political action committees? 
Would you also be able 
to furnish me with any statutes and regulations which explain any 
reporting requirements that your state may impose on political 
action committees? 
Any other information would also be helpful. 
I'm aware that I may have misdirected this inquiry. 
If so, I hope you 
will excuse the error and forward my request to the proper authority. 
Thank you very much for your help. 
RPH:pjg

SmithKline Corporation is in the process of establishing a system of

sections.conclusion: There is nothing in the Act which prohibits use of a payroll deduction system for the making of contributions to a political action committee. However, transactions related to California campaigns must be reported in accordance with the Act. A committee will incur a reporting obligation when its California activity reaches $500. Sources of all contributions of $50 or more must be itemized by name of donor, address, etc.
sections.question_synthetic: The inquirer asks whether California law permits payroll deduction systems for political action committee contributions and what reporting requirements apply to such committees.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC confirms that payroll deduction systems for PAC contributions are permitted under California law. However, the committee must comply with reporting requirements, including itemizing all contributions of $50 or more once California activity reaches $500.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request (dated February 21, 1978) and the FPPC response (dated March 14, 1976 - note appears to be a dating error in OCR). Some OCR artifacts present but content is generally clear. Response letter is from FPPC official (signature partially illegible).

CONCLUSION: There is nothing in the Act which prohibits use of a payroll deduction system for the making of contributions to a political action committee. However, transactions related to California campaigns must be reported in accordance with the Act. A committee will incur a reporting obligation when its California activity reaches $500. Sources of all contributions of $50 or more must be itemized by name of donor, address, etc.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to SmithKline Corporation's inquiry about using payroll deductions for political action committee contributions in California, confirming this practice is permitted but subject to state reporting requirements when California-related activities reach $500.
